items.reality: It's O(1) **on average**. Pathological hash collisions degrade to O(n) per operation, though CPython's hashing and table-growth strategy make this very rare in real workloads.
items.example:
```python
# Average case: instant
large[some_key]

# Worst case (artificial): every key hashes the same -> O(n) per lookup
```

items.reality: Since Python 3.7, dicts preserve insertion order as a language guarantee, so iteration is deterministic.

items.myth: Any object can be a dict key.
items.reality: Only hashable objects qualify. Built-in mutable containers (list/dict/set) raise `TypeError: unhashable type` when used as keys.

items.myth: A dict and an OrderedDict are interchangeable.
items.reality: Both preserve insertion order now, but OrderedDict adds order-aware operations (`move_to_end`, equality that compares order). For plain ordered storage, a regular dict is enough.
items.example:
```python
from collections import OrderedDict
OrderedDict([(1,'a'),(2,'b')]) == OrderedDict([(2,'b'),(1,'a')])  # False
{1:'a',2:'b'} == {2:'b',1:'a'}                                  # True
```
